name: Czechia
summary: A growing Prague/Brno tech hub with low flat-ish tax, cheap or moderately priced English MSc options and dual citizenship allowed makes Czechia a real study->employee-card route for two engineers; but the lack of a Dhaka mission (all long-stay visas decided in New Delhi under a 50-application quota), a slow ~10-year citizenship clock with B1 Czech, only a 9-month post-study job-search window, a thin Bangladeshi/Muslim community and a new Babis anti-migration coalition temper it.

links.title: Study in Czechia (official national portal)
Immigration Portal of the Czech Republic (ipc.gov.cz, official)

job-market.summary: Booming Prague/Brno tech hub; ~15,000 new IT roles in 2025, ~190k devs, demand outstrips supply for both engineers.
job-market.reasoning: Czechia's IT market grew ~18% in 2025 with roughly 15,000 new tech roles, software/data/AI engineers top the in-demand list, and Brno alone hosts ~45,000-50,000 IT professionals while graduating only ~3,500 against ~4,200 needed. With ~190,000 developers and demand exceeding supply, both CS graduates are readily absorbed, and English-speaking tech scenes exist at Microsoft, Google, Red Hat and many product firms. Slightly below top-tier because the absolute market is smaller than Germany/UK and some local roles still expect Czech.
evidence: ~18% IT-role growth and ~15,000 new tech jobs in 2025 (Nucamp)
Brno: ~45,000-50,000 IT pros; ~3,500 grads vs ~4,200 needed
~190,000 developers nationally; demand outstrips supply
Software devs, data scientists, AI/ML engineers top demand 2025
2026 Blue Card threshold may drop to 1.2x avg wage for shortage tech

links.title: Most In-Demand Tech Job in Czech Republic 2025 (Nucamp)
Czechia's 2026 visa changes for non-EU workers (Expats.cz)

visa-access.summary: No Dhaka mission; all long-stay visas decided in New Delhi under a 50-application quota with BD-specific delays.
visa-access.reasoning: There is no Czech embassy in Dhaka: Bangladeshi nationals must apply for long-stay study/work visas at the Czech Embassy in New Delhi, and 2026 rules require applying from the home-country mission. New Delhi caps Bangladesh-region long-term intake at 50 applications per registration window (at least 20 reserved for family reunification, max 5 for seasonal work), and appointments for Bangladeshi nationals are deliberately assigned with 1-2 months' advance notice 'due to the current situation' in Bangladesh, plus applicants must show 2 years' residence proof. This New-Delhi routing, tight quota and BD-specific friction place it near the severe-barrier band.
evidence: No Czech mission in Dhaka; long-stay visas decided in New Delhi
Registration windows 2026: May 4, Jun 1, Jul 7, Aug 3, Sep 7 (New Delhi)
Quota: 50 applications/period; >=20 family reunion, <=5 seasonal
Bangladeshi appointments given 1-2 months' notice 'due to situation'
2026 rule: non-EU must apply from home-country embassy only

links.title: Long-term Visa Appointments for Bangladesh etc. (Czech Embassy New Delhi, official)
Czechia's 2026 visa changes for non-EU workers (Expats.cz)

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship allowed (since 2014) and BD permits dual with EU, but ~10yr clock + B1 Czech + integration test.
citizenship.reasoning: Czechia has permitted dual citizenship since 1 January 2014, and Bangladesh allows dual nationality with EU states, so this couple could naturalise without renouncing Bangladeshi citizenship (a genuine plus). However the timeline is long: naturalisation requires either 5 years of permanent residence (itself reached only after ~5 years of temporary residence) or 10 years' total legal residence, plus a B1 Czech exam, an integration/civics test and self-sufficiency. The ~10-year effective path and B1 Czech bar hold it at the mixed band despite the dual-citizenship advantage.
evidence: Dual citizenship allowed since 01.01.2014; original nationality retained
Bangladesh permits dual nationality with EU countries (DNC)
Naturalisation: 5yr permanent residence OR 10yr total legal residence
B1 Czech exam (ALTE-certified) + Czech-realities civics test required
Must prove self-sufficiency, no tax/fine debts; MoI decides in 180 days

links.title: Conferment of Czech citizenship (portal.gov.cz, official)
Citizenship (cizinci.cz, official integration portal)

post-study-work.summary: 9-month post-study job-seeker stay, then in-country switch to Employee Card/Blue Card; spouse can work.
post-study-work.reasoning: Non-EU graduates of Czech universities may stay up to 9 months on a job-seeker / business-purpose long-term residence to find qualified work, then switch in-country to an Employee Card (valid up to 2 years, renewable) or an EU Blue Card. This is meaningfully shorter than the 18-36 month windows in Germany, Australia or Canada, and the Employee Card still involves an employer contract and qualification check. Adequate but short, so it sits in the lower-middle band; the 2026 90-day Employee Card processing guarantee helps.
evidence: 9-month post-study stay to seek employment (since 2019)
Switch in-country to Employee Card (up to 2 yrs, renewable) or Blue Card
Employee Card needs employment contract + qualification proof
From 01.01.2026 Employee Card applications must be decided in 90 days
Shorter than DE 18mo / AU-CA multi-year post-study windows

links.title: Work while or after studying (Study in Czechia, official)
Czechia's 2026 visa changes for non-EU workers (Expats.cz)

spouse-family.summary: Both can hold own permits and work; spouse of a worker can work, but study-phase dependent work is not automatic.
spouse-family.reasoning: As two qualified engineers, the strongest path is for each to hold an independent student then Employee Card/Blue Card permit, so both work freely in the work phase. A spouse joining a Blue Card or Employee Card holder via family reunification (after the sponsor's ~6 months' residence for Employee Card) can generally work without a separate permit. The weaker point is the study phase: a 2023 amendment abolished adult dependants' family-reunion-by-study route, so during the MSc the partner should ideally enrol or hold their own permit rather than rely on a dependant visa. Strong overall but not the seamless both-work-in-every-phase top anchor.
evidence: Each partner can hold own student/Employee Card/Blue Card permit
Blue Card/Employee Card family reunion lets spouse work without extra permit
Employee Card sponsor needs ~6 months' residence to reunite family
2023 amendment abolished adult dependant family-reunion via study
Best for this couple: both enrol/work on their own permits, not dependant

links.title: Long-term Residence for Family Reunification (ipc.gov.cz, official)
Family member in Czechia (European Commission, official)

msc-study.summary: English-taught CS MSc widely available (~EUR 2,500-10,000/yr); free only in Czech; students may work, spouse can join.
msc-study.reasoning: Charles University, CTU Prague and Masaryk (Brno) offer English-taught CS Master's, but English programmes charge tuition typically ~EUR 2,500-10,000 per year (some technical fields higher); only Czech-language programmes at public universities are tuition-free, and Czech is hard to reach at entry level. Students may work and a spouse can accompany. Solidly good given the low cost of living and strong tech ecosystem, but not the near-free abundant-English profile of Germany.
evidence: English-taught Master's tuition ~EUR 2,500-10,000/yr (public unis)
Tuition-free only for Czech-language programmes at public universities
Charles University, CTU Prague, Masaryk offer English CS Master's
Students may work; spouse may accompany
Lower living costs than Western Europe ease total study budget

links.title: Tuition fees (Study in Czechia, official)
Master's Degrees in the Czech Republic 2026 (Study.eu)

pr-pathway.summary: PR after 5 years' continuous residence, but study time counts only half; A2 Czech and clean record required.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Third-country nationals can obtain permanent residence after 5 years of continuous temporary residence, which is a clear standard route. The catch for this couple is that time on a student residence permit counts only at half value, so a 2-year MSc adds only 1 year - they would need ~4 more years on an Employee Card/Blue Card to reach 5, making the realistic PR horizon ~6 years from arrival. An A2 Czech exam and clean financial/criminal record are also required. Standard-paced and predictable, hence the middle band.
evidence: PR after 5 years' continuous residence (ipc.gov.cz)
Student-permit time counts at HALF toward the 5 years
MSc 2 yrs = ~1 yr credit; then ~4 yrs Employee/Blue Card needed
A2 Czech exam required for permanent residence
Blue Card: 2 yrs in CZ + 1 yr in another EU state can combine

links.title: Permanent Residence after 5 Years of Temporary Residence (ipc.gov.cz, official)

income-cost.summary: SW median ~CZK 1.4M (~EUR 56k), Prague ~CZK 1.68M (~EUR 67k); low tax + cheaper rents let two incomes save.
income-cost.reasoning: Median software-engineer total comp is ~CZK 1,402,000 (~EUR 56,000), rising to ~CZK 1,678,000 (~EUR 67,000) in Prague and to CZK 2-3M at top firms (Microsoft, Pure Storage). Gross pay is below Western Europe, but a low ~15% flat-ish income tax, ~11.6% employee contributions and far cheaper housing than Berlin (single-person costs ~EUR 780/month excl. rent; Prague 1-bed ~EUR 700-1,200) mean two engineering incomes save well, with Brno ~13% cheaper than Prague. Net savings potential is good rather than exceptional given lower headline salaries.
evidence: Median SW total comp ~CZK 1,402,000 (~EUR 56k) (Levels.fyi, Jun 2026)
Prague ~CZK 1,678,000 (~EUR 67k); top firms CZK 2-3M
Single-person monthly cost ~EUR 780 excl. rent (Numbeo)
Prague 1-bed ~EUR 700-1,200/month; Brno ~13% cheaper than Prague
Low ~15% tax + ~11.6% contributions preserve take-home

links.title: Software Engineer Salary in Czech Republic (Levels.fyi)
Cost of Living in Prague, Jun 2026 (Numbeo)

healthcare.summary: Universal public insurance (VZP) for employees; good quality, but non-EU students must buy commercial cover.
healthcare.reasoning: Czechia runs a universal public health insurance system (VZP covers ~6 million people) giving employed residents comprehensive, good-quality access at modest cost (employee 4.5% of gross). The gap for this couple is the study phase: non-EU students cannot join public VZP unless employed and must buy commercial foreigners' insurance, and health cover is mandatory for the visa/residence permit. Quality and cost are solid once both are working, so it sits in the decent-with-newcomer-gaps band.
evidence: Universal public insurance; VZP covers ~6M (largest insurer)
Employees join public insurance; employee contribution 4.5% of gross
Non-EU students must buy commercial insurance (not eligible for VZP)
Health insurance mandatory for stays over 90 days / residence permits
Good-quality access to GPs, specialists, hospitals once employed

culture-language.summary: English works in Prague/Brno tech, but Czech is needed for officialdom, A2 for PR and B1 for citizenship.
culture-language.reasoning: English is workable in Prague/Brno tech workplaces and among younger Czechs, and livability is high with low cost of living and central-Europe access. However Czech is a hard Slavic language needed for bureaucracy and daily life outside the hubs, with an A2 exam for permanent residence and a B1 exam plus civics test for citizenship. This local-language bar for officialdom and the couple's passport goal keeps it in the mixed band.
evidence: English usable in Prague/Brno tech and among younger Czechs
Czech required for officialdom and life outside major cities
A2 Czech exam for permanent residence; B1 for citizenship
Czech is a difficult Slavic language to acquire
High livability, low cost of living, central location

safety-law.summary: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: top-20 globally (ranked 20 of 140 on country profile); low crime, sound courts.
safety-law.reasoning: Czechia ranks in the global top 20 of the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (its official country profile cites rank 20 of 140; the full 2025 index covers 143 jurisdictions), placing it 15th among EU/EFTA/North America. Violent crime is low and courts are broadly sound. Slightly below Germany/Nordics because of a less robust experience for visible-minority migrants and rising anti-migrant rhetoric.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: global top 20 (rank 20 of 140 on profile)
15th of 31 in EU/EFTA/North America region
2025 index covers 143 countries/jurisdictions, 0-1 scale
Low violent crime; functional, broadly independent courts
Migrant experience weaker amid anti-immigrant political climate

politics.summary: New Babis coalition (Dec 2025) with anti-migrant SPD pledges zero-tolerance migration; IT visas pragmatically kept.
politics.reasoning: Andrej Babis was sworn in as PM on 9 December 2025 leading an ANO coalition with the anti-migrant SPD and the Motorists, holding 108 of 200 seats and winning confidence in January 2026 on a 'zero-tolerance for illegal migration' platform, a new Migration Act and resistance to the EU Migration Pact. Babis's previous government did expand fast-track IT visa schemes, so skilled-tech routes are likely preserved by economic pragmatism, but coalition tension and a tightening, anti-immigrant tone weigh the score down toward the lower-middle band.
evidence: Babis sworn in as PM 09.12.2025; ANO + SPD + Motorists, 108/200 seats
Government won confidence vote January 2026 (108-91)
Platform: zero-tolerance migration, new Migration Act, resist EU Pact
Babis's prior govt expanded fast-track IT visa schemes (pragmatism)
Coalition tensions and 2026 municipal elections add volatility

tax.summary: Low flat-ish tax: 15% up to ~CZK 1.76M, 23% above; employee contributions ~11.6%; strong take-home.
tax.reasoning: Czechia taxes personal income at just 15% up to ~CZK 1,762,812 (~EUR 71,000) in 2026 and 23% only above that, with employee social and health contributions of ~11.6% (6.5% pension, 0.6% sickness, 4.5% health). For typical software salaries the all-in effective burden is roughly 26-34%, clearly lighter than Germany's 35-40%, giving these engineers a strong take-home. Among the better tax environments in this set.
evidence: Income tax 15% up to ~CZK 1,762,812 (~EUR 71k), 23% above (2026)
Employee contributions ~11.6% (6.5% pension, 0.6% sickness, 4.5% health)
No cap on health-insurance contributions; social-security cap CZK 2,350,416
Effective all-in burden ~26-34% for SW salaries
Lighter than Germany's ~35-40% deductions

muslim-diaspora.summary: Very small Muslim community (~20k, 0.2%), few mosques, limited halal, and notable Islamophobia.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Czechia has only ~20,000 Muslims (~0.2% of the population), no large purpose-built mosques (worship is largely in small/basement prayer spaces and only ~3 recognised mosques), and limited visible halal infrastructure outside Prague. The Bangladeshi/South Asian community is negligible, and Islamophobic incidents and political hostility are well documented, with Muslims among the least-accepted groups. This is a weak environment for a Muslim Bangladeshi couple seeking community.
evidence: ~20,000 Muslims (~0.2% of population)
No large purpose-built mosques; ~3 recognised, plus basement prayer rooms
Limited halal availability outside Prague
Negligible Bangladeshi/South Asian community
Documented Islamophobia; Muslims among least-accepted groups
